questions.statement:
<p><u style="font-weight: 700;">Direction</u><span style="font-weight: 700;">: Choose the correct alternative which can be substituted for the given sentence.</span></p>

<p>One who doesn&#39;t consume alcohol<span style="white-space:pre"> </span></p>

<p><strong>Choose&nbsp;the&nbsp;option&nbsp;that&nbsp;is&nbsp;closest&nbsp;in&nbsp;meaning&nbsp;to&nbsp;the&nbsp;highlighted&nbsp;word&nbsp;in&nbsp;the&nbsp;given&nbsp;sentence:</strong></p>

<p>Don&rsquo;t&nbsp;try&nbsp;to&nbsp;cheat&nbsp;me&nbsp;by&nbsp;<strong>concocting</strong>&nbsp;a&nbsp;cock&nbsp;and&nbsp;bull&nbsp;story.&nbsp;</p>

<p><strong>Select the option that expresses the given sentence in direct speech.</strong><br />
Thomas Muller exclaimed with joy that Germany had won the tournament.&nbsp;</p>
options: Thomas Muller said, &ldquo;Germany has won the tournament.&rdquo;&nbsp; /
Thomas Muller said, &ldquo;Wow! Germany won the tournament.&rdquo;&nbsp; /
Thomas Muller exclaimed, &ldquo;Hurrah! Germany has won the tournament!&rdquo;&nbsp; /
Thomas Muller said, &ldquo;Germany won the tournament.&rdquo; /

questions.statement:
<p>Read the following sentence and identify the part that has an error.</p>

<p>Madhu said to her friend, / &ldquo;Have you been ever / to a big city/ like Mumbai?&rdquo;</p>

<p>Select the most appropriate meaning of the given idiom.</p>

<p>A slap on the wrist</p>
options: Beating someone too much on the wrist. /

questions.statement:
<p>The following sentence has been split into four segments. Identify the segment that contains an error.</p>

<p>A Himalayas stretch across / the north-east part / of India and pass / through many countries.</p>

<p>The following sentence has been split into four segments. Identify the segment that contains a grammatical error.</p>

<p>We must / take care / of one other / in times of crisis.</p>

<p><span style="font-weight: 700; white-space: pre-wrap;">In the following question, out of the four alternatives, select the alternative which will improve the underlined part of the sentence. In case no improvement is needed, select &ldquo;No improvement&rdquo;.</span></p>

<div>Marie asked me to <u>look into</u>&nbsp;for a while as she was caught up in a task.</div>

<p>Select the most appropriate option that rectifies the underlined group of words in the given sentence.</p>

<p data-path-to-node="16">Neither the lead architect <u><b data-index-in-node="27" data-path-to-node="16">nor the junior engineers, despite having worked</b>&nbsp;</u><strong><u>through the weekend, was</u></strong> able to identify the flaw in the load-bearing calculations.</p>
options: or the junior engineers, despite having worked&nbsp;through the weekend, were /
nor the junior engineers, despite having worked&nbsp;for the weekend, was /
nor the junior engineers, despite having worked&nbsp;through the weekend, were /
nor the junior engineers, despite having worked&nbsp;for the weekend, were /
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is '3' i.e. nor the junior engineers, despite having worked through the weekend, were .

Key Points

  • The subject in the sentence is compound: "Neither the lead architect nor the junior engineers." In such cases, the verb agrees with the subject closer to it. Here, "junior engineers" is plural, so the verb should be "were" instead of "was."
  • The phrase "worked through the weekend" is correct and needs no change, as it refers to working continuously during the weekend.
  • "Nor" is the correct conjunction to pair with "neither" in the sentence.

Correct sentence: Neither the lead architect nor the junior engineers, despite having worked through the weekend, were able to identify the flaw in the load-bearing calculations.

Additional Information

  • Subject-Verb Agreement:
    • When the subject is compound, the verb agrees with the subject closer to it.
    • Example: Neither the teacher nor the students were excited about the event.
  • Conjunction Pair:
    • "Neither" is always paired with "nor" in English grammar.
    • Example: Neither he nor she wants to attend the meeting.

questions.statement:
<p>In the following question, out of the four given options, select the one that best expresses the meaning of the idiom/phrase.</p>

<p>Bide one&#39;s time</p>
options: To happens quite often, but not all the time&nbsp; /
To not accept any kind of antisocial behaviour&nbsp; /
To wait quietly for a good opportunity to do something&nbsp; /

questions.statement: Select the correctly spelt word.

<p>By living in a rural area with access to the city, she gets _____.</p>
options: to keep an ear to the ground /
in for a penny in for a pound /
